source.title: Central cancellation of self-produced tickle sensation.

confidence_rationale: fMRI evidence that self-generated tactile stimulation produces attenuated somatosensory cortex response relative to identical externally-generated stimulation, localising the cancellation to a cerebellar forward-model signal. This is the neural signature of the comparator operating: the cerebellum predicts, the predicted consequences are subtracted, and both S1 and cerebellar activity drop when prediction matches observation. For REE, the paper supplies the neural mechanism that underwrites Shergill 2003's behavioural attenuation. Confidence high because the paradigm is clean and the finding has been replicated across multiple studies.
failure_signatures: Tickle stimulation is innocuous tactile input, not nociception. Whether the same cerebellar forward-model cancellation operates for harm signals is assumed but not shown.

The cancellation is strongest when self-generation is temporally immediate; even small introduced delays (100-200 ms) restore the tickle sensation. This suggests the comparator has a narrow temporal window, which REE must match -- z_harm_s_pred and z_harm_s_obs need to be aligned at the correct timestep.

The cerebellar role suggests specialised hardware, not a generic neural-network function. An REE implementation that tries to learn E2_harm_s end-to-end from a general architecture may struggle to match biological performance without inductive biases analogous to cerebellar circuit structure.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Self-produced tactile stimulation on the palm is perceived as less ticklish than identical stimulation generated by an external source (a robot). fMRI showed reduced activity in somatosensory cortex and cerebellum when the stimulus was self-generated. Introducing a small temporal delay between the self-movement and the stimulus restored both the ticklish perception and the neural response. The cerebellar activity is interpreted as the signal that cancels the sensory response to self-generated stimulation by predicting its consequences.
mapping.ree_translation: The E2_harm_s + comparator architecture in the SD-003 successor is the computational analogue of the cerebellar forward model. The REE equivalent of the S1 attenuation is a reduction in the magnitude of the z_harm_s_obs - z_harm_s_pred residual when E2_harm_s is accurate. The tickle delay experiment is especially important for REE: it says the comparator has a narrow temporal window, which means E2_harm_s predictions must be aligned at the correct timestep. A one-step mis-alignment breaks the cancellation. This sets an implementation constraint: the comparator is computed per-timestep on matched (pred, obs) pairs, not on cumulative sums.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Blakemore 1998 is about innocuous tactile stimulation (tickling the palm). Nociception is a different modality with different ascending pathways (spinothalamic vs lemniscal). The transfer assumes the cerebellar forward-model mechanism generalises across modalities. There is secondary evidence this is true (for instance, Blakemore et al 1999 for proprioception), but the specific harm extension has not been directly tested in humans.
mapping.source_context: Nature Neuroscience paper from the Wolpert / Frith collaboration, one of the foundational empirical papers behind the comparator model. The tickle paradigm became canonical because its behavioural contrast (self vs external) is strong, intuitively clear, and the neural correlate is robust in fMRI.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Nature Neuroscience, foundational fMRI study, replicated many times). Mapping fidelity moderate-high because the mechanism (cerebellar forward model cancelling reafference) is directly what ARC-033's E2_harm_s is supposed to implement; the main gap is the modality (tactile vs nociceptive). Transfer risk moderate-low: the forward-model motif is well-established across modalities, and REE assumes modality generality in ARC-033 anyway.
